steps.title: De Bank
steps.desc: Money makes the world go round — and in the Netherlands, it's almost entirely digital. Cash is disappearing, everyone 'pint', and the Tikkie app rules social life. Let's learn the vocabulary of Dutch banking.

steps.example: Ik heb een rekening bij de Rabobank.
steps.exampleEn: I have an account at Rabobank.
steps.note:
De-word. Polysemy!
'De bankrekening' = bank account.
'De rekening' (in restaurant) = the bill.
'De rekening betalen' = to pay the bill.

Word family: from 'rekenen' (to calculate/count).
'Rekenen' = the school subject for arithmetic.
'Wiskunde' = mathematics (the advanced subject).
Dutch kids learn 'rekenen' first (1+1=2),
then 'wiskunde' later (algebra, geometry).
So 'de rekening' literally = 'the calculation'.

steps.example: Ik spaar elke maand honderd euro.
steps.exampleEn: I save a hundred euros every month.
steps.note:
'De spaarrekening' = savings account.
'Het spaargeld' = savings.
The Dutch are known for being 'zuinig' (thrifty).

steps.en: to pay by card / to use debit card
steps.exampleEn: Can I pay by card here?
steps.note:
THE most Dutch payment word.
'Pinnen' = to pay with your bankpas (debit card).
'De pinpas' / 'de bankpas' = debit card.
Cash is almost gone in NL — everything is pinnen.

steps.example: Ik maak het geld vandaag over.
steps.exampleEn: I'll transfer the money today.
steps.note:
Separable verb: over|maken.
'Geld overmaken' = to transfer money.
'De overschrijving' = the bank transfer.
Dutch people transfer money for EVERYTHING.

steps.example: Betaalt u contant of met de pin?
steps.exampleEn: Are you paying cash or by card?
steps.note:
'Contant betalen' = to pay in cash.
'Contant geld' = cash money.
Many Dutch shops now say 'alleen pinnen'
= card only. Cash is dying in NL.

steps.exampleEn: I've lost my debit card.
steps.note:
De-word. Also: 'de pinpas'.
NOT a credit card! Dutch use debit cards.
'Creditcard' exists but is much less common.
'Kwijt zijn' = to have lost something.

steps.title: 💳 Pinnen: The Dutch Way
steps.text:
The Netherlands is one of the most cashless countries in the world.

Kan ik pinnen? = Can I pay by card?
Alleen pinnen. = Card only.
Contactloos betalen. = Contactless payment.

The Tikkie app: Dutch people send each other
payment requests for tiny amounts.
Shared a pizza? You'll get a Tikkie for €4,50.
This is NOT rude — it's normal and expected.

'Going Dutch' (splitting the bill) is literally
the Dutch way of life. Nobody pays for someone
else unless it's a special occasion.

steps.example: Ik moet geld opnemen bij de automaat.
steps.exampleEn: I need to withdraw money at the ATM.
steps.note:
Separable: op|nemen.
'De geldautomaat' / 'de pinautomaat' = ATM.
ATMs are getting rarer — most people never use cash.

steps.example: Hij heeft een lening bij de bank.
steps.exampleEn: He has a loan from the bank.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Lenen' = to borrow OR to lend.
'Geld lenen' = to borrow money.
'Geld uitlenen' = to lend money.
Context tells you which!

steps.hint: Bankrekening (account) vs rekening in a restaurant (bill)

steps.s: Kan ik hier ___? (pay by card)
steps.hint: The Dutch word for paying by debit card
€4,50 for your share of the pizza

steps.s: Ik maak het geld vandaag ___.
steps.hint: Overmaken is separable: ik maak ... over

steps.s: Ik heb een {1} bij de Rabobank. Ik {2} elke maand honderd euro.
steps.hint: I have an account. I save 100 euros monthly.

steps.title: Rekeningen & Verzekering
steps.desc: Bills, insurance, taxes — the less glamorous side of living in the Netherlands. But you need these words to survive. Every Dutch person deals with huur, verzekering, and belasting.

steps.example: De huur is zeshonderd euro per maand.
steps.exampleEn: The rent is six hundred euros per month.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Huren' = to rent.
'De huurder' = the tenant.
'De verhuurder' = the landlord.
'Per maand' = per month.

steps.example: De zorgverzekering is verplicht in Nederland.
steps.exampleEn: Health insurance is mandatory in the Netherlands.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Verzekeren' = to insure.
'De zorgverzekering' = health insurance (MANDATORY).
Everyone in NL must have one. ~€130/month.

steps.exampleEn: My phone subscription costs thirty euros.
steps.note:
Het-word. Phone, gym, streaming, transport...
'Een abonnement nemen' = to take out a subscription.
'Opzeggen' = to cancel a subscription.

steps.example: In Nederland betaal je veel belasting.
steps.exampleEn: In the Netherlands you pay a lot of tax.
steps.note:
De-word. 'De Belastingdienst' = the Tax Authority.
'Aangifte doen' = to file your tax return.
'De inkomstenbelasting' = income tax.
Dutch taxes are high but fund excellent services.

steps.exampleEn: There is a twenty percent discount.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Korting krijgen' = to get a discount.
'Uitverkoop' = sale (clearance).
Dutch people love korting — zuinig!

steps.example: De factuur moet binnen dertig dagen betaald worden.
steps.exampleEn: The invoice must be paid within thirty days.
steps.note:
De-word. Formal: factuur. Informal: rekening/bon.
'Een factuur sturen' = to send an invoice.
'De bon' = the receipt (at a shop).

steps.exampleEn: He has student debt.
steps.note:
De-word. Also means 'guilt/fault' — same word!
'Het is mijn schuld.' = It's my fault.
'Schulden hebben' = to have debts.
Related: 'de lening' = the loan.

steps.exampleEn: The price includes VAT.
steps.note:
Stands for 'belasting over de toegevoegde waarde'.
Dutch VAT = 21% (standard) or 9% (food, books).
'Inclusief btw' / 'exclusief btw'.

steps.title: 💡 Per Maand, Per Jaar
steps.text:
de huur = €800 per maand (per month)
de verzekering = €130 per maand
het abonnement = €30 per maand
de belasting = per jaar (per year)

Useful phrases:
'Hoeveel kost dat per maand?' = How much per month?
'Dat is inclusief/exclusief...' = incl./excl.
'Maandelijks' = monthly
'Jaarlijks' = yearly / annually

steps.q: Health insurance in the Netherlands is:

steps.s: Er is twintig procent ___.

steps.hint: Hij heeft schulden. Het is mijn schuld.

steps.s: De prijs is inclusief ___. (VAT)
De {1} is zeshonderd euro per maand. De {2} is verplicht in Nederland.
steps.hint: The rent is €600. Health insurance is mandatory.

steps.title: Zou Je...?
steps.desc: Meet 'zou' — the Swiss Army knife of Dutch. It makes requests polite, advice gentle, and opens the door to hypothetical thinking. This is the word that separates A2 Dutch from B1 Dutch.

steps.title: 🔑 What Is 'Zou'?
steps.text:
'Zou' is the past tense of 'zullen' (shall/will).
But it's NOT about the past!

Zou/zouden = would / could / should

It has THREE main uses:

1. POLITE REQUESTS:
Zou je me willen helpen? = Would you help me?
(Much softer than: Wil je me helpen?)

2. SOFTENED ADVICE:
Ik zou een paraplu meenemen. = I would take an umbrella.
(Softer than: Neem een paraplu mee!)

3. HYPOTHETICAL:
Als ik rijk was, zou ik reizen.
= If I were rich, I would travel.

All three make language MORE POLITE or LESS CERTAIN.

steps.title: Zou/Zouden
steps.label: Past tense of zullen
steps.note:
Simple: zou (singular) / zouden (plural + jullie).
Same pattern as kon/konden, moest/moesten.
Infinitive goes to the END:
'Ik zou willen helpen.' (I would like to help.)

steps.en: would you like to / would you...
steps.exampleEn: Would you help me?
steps.note:
POLITE REQUEST formula.
'Zou je...' = would you...
+ willen + infinitive.
Much softer than 'Wil je...'!

steps.exampleEn: I would take an umbrella.
steps.note:
SOFTENED ADVICE.
'Ik zou...' = I would... (if I were you).
Implied: 'Als ik jou was, zou ik...'
= If I were you, I would...

steps.exampleEn: It could rain. / It might rain.
steps.note:
'Zou kunnen' = might / could (possibility).
'Het zou kunnen dat...' = It could be that...
Expresses uncertainty — very useful for B1!

steps.title: 📐 Polite Scale
steps.text:
From blunt to very polite:

Help me! → Help me!
Wil je me helpen? → Do you want to help me?
Kun je me helpen? → Can you help me?
Zou je me willen helpen? → Would you help me?
Zou u me willen helpen? → Would you help me? (formal)
Zou u zo vriendelijk willen zijn om...? → (ultra-formal)

'Zou' + 'u' = maximum politeness.
Use this with strangers, officials, older people.

At work, 'Zou je...' is the standard polite form.

steps.q: 'Zou je me willen helpen?' is more polite than:
steps.hint: Zou + willen is softer than wil

steps.s: ___ je het raam willen opendoen?

steps.hint: I WOULD take an umbrella (if I were you)

steps.s: Het ___ kunnen regenen. (might)
steps.hint: It WOULD be able to = it might

steps.s: {1} je me {2} helpen? Ik {3} een jas meenemen.
steps.hint: Would you help me? I would take a jacket.

steps.title: Als Ik Zou...
steps.desc: Now zou gets creative. Hypothetical situations: 'If I were rich...', 'If I could fly...'. Plus wishes with 'zou willen dat'. This is where Dutch opens up to imagination and nuance.

steps.title: 📐 Hypothetical Conditionals
steps.text:
The pattern:
ALS + imperfectum (simple past), ZOU + infinitive.

Als ik rijk was, zou ik een huis kopen.
= If I were rich, I would buy a house.

Als hij hier woonde, zou hij fietsen.
= If he lived here, he would cycle.

Als we meer tijd hadden, zouden we blijven.
= If we had more time, we would stay.

The als-clause uses imperfectum (Unit 10!).
The main clause uses zou/zouden + infinitive.

This is EXACTLY like English:
If I WERE rich (past), I WOULD buy (conditional).

⚠️ Dutch also allows: Als ik rijk ZOU ZIJN...
Both forms are correct and common.

steps.example: Als ik president was, zou ik alles veranderen.
steps.exampleEn: If I were president, I would change everything.
steps.note:
Two options for the als-clause:
1. 'Als ik rijk WAS...' (imperfectum — common)
2. 'Als ik rijk ZOU ZIJN...' (zou — also fine)
Both are correct. #1 is more spoken.

steps.en: I would like that / I wish that
steps.example: Ik zou willen dat ik meer tijd had.
steps.exampleEn: I wish I had more time.
steps.note:
Wish formula: 'Ik zou willen dat...'
The dat-clause uses imperfectum!
'Ik zou willen dat het ZOMER WAS.'
= I wish it were summer.

steps.en: it could be that / it's possible that
steps.example: Het zou kunnen dat hij ziek is.
steps.exampleEn: It could be that he's sick.
steps.note:
Uncertainty formula.
'Het zou kunnen dat...' = It's possible that...
'Misschien' = maybe (simpler alternative).
'Zou kunnen' = softer, more uncertain than 'kan'.

steps.title: 🧰 The Full Zou Toolkit
steps.text:
1. POLITE REQUEST: Zou je... willen...?
   Zou je de deur willen sluiten?

2. SOFTENED ADVICE: Ik zou...
   Ik zou een arts bezoeken.

3. HYPOTHETICAL: Als... was, zou...
   Als ik jou was, zou ik gaan.

4. WISH: Ik zou willen dat...
   Ik zou willen dat het vrijdag was.

5. UNCERTAINTY: Het zou kunnen dat...
   Het zou kunnen dat het morgen regent.

6. REPORTED SPEECH (preview for Unit 18):
   Hij zei dat hij zou komen.
   = He said he would come.

You now have all 6 uses.
Uses 1-5 are active now. #6 comes in Unit 18.

steps.q: 'Als ik rijk was, zou ik reizen.' — the als-clause uses:

steps.s: Ik ___ willen dat ik meer tijd had.

steps.q: 'Het zou kunnen dat hij ziek is' expresses:

steps.s: Als ik president {1}, {2} ik alles veranderen.
steps.hint: If I WERE president, I WOULD change everything.

steps.s: Als we meer tijd hadden, ___ we blijven.

steps.q: Both 'Als ik rijk was' and 'Als ik rijk zou zijn' are:
steps.hint: Two valid forms for hypothetical als-clauses

steps.title: Winkelen & Klagen
steps.desc: Something's broken, the wrong size, or just not what you expected. Time to learn the Dutch art of complaining and returning items — politely, of course. This is where zou-politeness meets real life.

steps.exampleEn: I want to file a complaint.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Klagen' = to complain.
'Een klacht indienen' = to file a complaint.
'De klachtenservice' = complaints department.

steps.example: Kan ik dit ruilen voor een andere maat?
steps.exampleEn: Can I exchange this for a different size?
steps.note:
'Ruilen' = to exchange (like for like).
'Omruilen' = also exchange.
Different from 'terugbrengen' (return for refund).

steps.exampleEn: I want to return this.
steps.note:
Separable: terug|brengen.
'Ik breng het terug.' = I bring it back.
'Terugbetalen' = to refund.
Keep the bon (receipt)!

steps.exampleEn: The washing machine is broken.
steps.note:
Adjective, no inflection needed.
'Kapotmaken' = to break (on purpose or by accident).
Very common everyday word.

steps.exampleEn: The package was damaged.
steps.note:
More formal than 'kapot'.
'Beschadigen' = to damage.
'De schade' = the damage.
'Schade claimen' = to claim damage.

steps.title: 🛍️ In de Winkel: Ruilen
steps.text:
You: Goedemiddag. Ik zou dit graag willen ruilen.
= Good afternoon. I would like to exchange this.

Winkelier: Wat is het probleem?
= What's the problem?

You: Het is kapot / de verkeerde maat / beschadigd.
= It's broken / the wrong size / damaged.

Winkelier: Heeft u de bon?
= Do you have the receipt?

You: Ja, hier is de bon.
= Yes, here's the receipt.

Winkelier: Dan kunt u het ruilen of uw geld terugkrijgen.
= Then you can exchange it or get your money back.

Notice: 'Ik zou graag willen...' = maximum politeness.
This is the zou-formula from L3 in real life!

steps.exampleEn: Do you still have the receipt?
steps.note:
De-word. Keep your bon!
Without it, returning is much harder.
'De kassabon' = cash register receipt.

steps.q: 'Ik zou dit graag willen ruilen' is:

steps.s: Ik wil dit ___. (return/bring back)
steps.hint: To return something to the shop

steps.s: De wasmachine is ___.
Ik {1} dit graag willen {2}. Heeft u de {3}?
steps.hint: I would like to exchange this. Do you have the receipt?

steps.title: Op het Gemeentehuis
steps.desc: Welcome to Dutch bureaucracy. The gemeentehuis (city hall) is where life in the Netherlands officially begins: registration, BSN number, passport, rijbewijs. Every expat knows this place.

steps.example: Ik woon in de gemeente Amsterdam.
steps.exampleEn: I live in the municipality of Amsterdam.
steps.note:
De-word. The unit of local government.
'Het gemeentehuis' = city hall.
Every person in NL is registered at a gemeente.

steps.exampleEn: You need to fill in this form.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Invullen' = to fill in.
'Een formulier invullen' = to fill in a form.
Dutch bureaucracy loves formulieren.

steps.exampleEn: Put your signature here.
steps.note:
De-word. hand + tekening = hand + drawing.
'Tekenen' / 'ondertekenen' = to sign.
'Hier tekenen, alstublieft.' = Sign here, please.

steps.exampleEn: What is your BSN number?
steps.note:
BSN = Burgerservicenummer.
Your Dutch social security number.
You need it for EVERYTHING: work, bank, doctor, tax.

steps.example: Ik moet me inschrijven bij de gemeente.
steps.exampleEn: I need to register at the municipality.
steps.note:
Separable: in|schrijven.
'Zich inschrijven' = to register (yourself).
'De inschrijving' = the registration.
First thing you do when moving to NL!

steps.exampleEn: May I see your driver's license?
steps.note:
Het-word. rij + bewijs = driving + proof.
'Een rijbewijs halen' = to get a driver's license.
'Het paspoort' = the passport.

steps.title: 🏛️ Bij de Gemeente: Inschrijven
steps.text:
Ambtenaar: Goedemorgen. Waarmee kan ik u helpen?
= Good morning. How can I help you?

Jij: Ik wil me graag inschrijven.
= I'd like to register, please.

Ambtenaar: Heeft u uw paspoort en huurcontract bij u?
= Do you have your passport and rental contract?

Jij: Ja, alstublieft.
= Yes, here you are.

Ambtenaar: Vult u dit formulier in, alstublieft.
Dan krijgt u een BSN-nummer.
= Fill in this form, please.
Then you'll get a BSN number.

Jij: Hoe lang duurt dat?
= How long does that take?

Ambtenaar: U krijgt het per post binnen twee weken.
= You'll receive it by mail within two weeks.

Notice: officials use 'u'. You use 'u' back.
This is formal Dutch in action.

steps.s: Ik moet me ___ bij de gemeente.
Zet hier uw ___.
U moet dit {1} invullen. Dan krijgt u een {2}.
steps.hint: Fill in this form. Then you get a BSN number.

steps.title: Zuinig Zijn
steps.desc: The Dutch have a reputation for being thrifty. Is it true? Let's explore the culture of money in the Netherlands: going Dutch, the Tikkie, tipping (or not), and why 'zuinig' isn't an insult.

steps.exampleEn: Dutch people are thrifty — and proud of it.
steps.note:
NOT an insult in Dutch! It's a virtue.
'Zuinig zijn' = to be careful with money.
'Zuinig omgaan met' = to handle carefully.
Opposite: 'verkwistend' = wasteful.

steps.example: Een fooi geven is niet verplicht.
steps.exampleEn: Tipping is not mandatory.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Een fooi geven' = to give a tip.
Dutch tipping: round up or 5-10% for good service.
NO American-style 20%. Wages are already decent.

steps.exampleEn: I'll send you a Tikkie.
steps.note:
THE Dutch payment app.
Split dinner? Tikkie for €12,50.
Shared a taxi? Tikkie for €3,75.
It's not stingy — it's Dutch!

steps.title: 📖 Lees: Geld in Nederland
steps.text:
Nederlanders staan bekend als zuinig.
Maar is dat waar?

Het is waar dat Nederlanders graag de rekening delen.
'Going Dutch' komt niet voor niets uit Nederland.
Als je met vrienden uit eten gaat,
betaalt iedereen zijn eigen deel.
Na het eten stuurt iemand een Tikkie.
Dat is heel normaal.

Fooi geven is niet verplicht.
In een restaurant kun je afronden of
5-10% extra geven voor goede service.
Maar niemand verwacht 20% zoals in Amerika.

Contant geld verdwijnt snel.
Bijna overal kun je pinnen.
Sommige winkels accepteren geen contant meer.
Nederland is bijna helemaal digitaal.

Zuinig zijn is geen schande in Nederland.
Het is slim. Het is praktisch.
En het is heel erg Nederlands.

deepDive.title: Vertaling
deepDive.text:
Dutch people are known as thrifty.
But is that true?

It's true that Dutch people like to split the bill.
'Going Dutch' doesn't come from the Netherlands for nothing.
When you go out to eat with friends,
everyone pays their own share.
After dinner, someone sends a Tikkie.
That's completely normal.

Tipping is not mandatory.
In a restaurant you can round up or
give 5-10% extra for good service.
But nobody expects 20% like in America.

Cash is disappearing fast.
Almost everywhere you can pay by card.
Some shops don't accept cash anymore.
The Netherlands is almost completely digital.

Being thrifty is not a shame in the Netherlands.
It's smart. It's practical.
And it's very Dutch.

steps.s: Een ___ geven is niet verplicht in Nederland.

steps.q: In the text, what is disappearing in the Netherlands?

steps.s: Ik stuur je een ___. (payment request)

steps.title: Unit 12 Review
steps.desc: Review time. Banking, zou/zouden, complaints, bureaucracy, and Dutch money culture. Let's bring it all together.

steps.s: Als ik rijk ___, zou ik een huis kopen.
Het ___ kunnen dat hij ziek is. (might)
steps.hint: It COULD be that... = zou kunnen

steps.s: Ik {1} dit graag willen {2}. Het is {3}.
steps.hint: I would like to exchange this. It's broken.

steps.s: Ik moet me ___ bij de gemeente.
___ ik jou was, zou ik een arts bezoeken.
Ik wil me graag {1}. Wat is mijn {2}?
steps.hint: I'd like to register. What is my BSN?
